narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70—which is a bit below the typical 3.21 average—and your Year 1 school decile ranking of 7th, which is stronger than the average 5.71. However, your LSAT score of 18 was notably lower than the dataset average of around 37, and your Year 3 decile dropped to 2nd, well below the typical 5.53. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 54% probability that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—a threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score, which carried the strongest weight in signaling potential challenges on the bar exam compared to what the model has seen in successful students. On a more positive note, your stronger Year 1 decile ranking worked against that failure prediction, pulling the odds in your favor by showing early academic promise above average. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA provided a helpful counterbalance, nudging the model toward expecting bar exam success despite being slightly below the norm. Overall, while your early performance and GPA were bright spots relative to typical applicants, the LSAT's dominant negative influence tipped the scales, making your predicted failure risk just high enough for denial. This doesn't reflect your full potential or character—many students overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, as that's the single biggest lever here. Strengthening your recent academic decile through targeted study or additional coursework could also help rebuild momentum. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation or relevant experience might address gaps in future applications. Finally, applying to programs with more holistic reviews or retake-friendly policies could open doors, giving you a clearer path to law school success.
